question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileIndex()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @Primary annotation on cacheManagerRecordFileIndex() to ensure this cache manager is selected by default for any unqualified @Cacheable annotation in the codebase, potentially causing authorization-related caches to use the 10-minute TTL instead of the intended shorter TTL, leading to stale approval data being used to authorize transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerToken()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CACHE_NAME_TOKEN_ACCOUNT cache (within CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN, expireAfterWrite=1s) to read a stale token account association record that shows a victim's account as still associated with a token (even after dissociation), causing the EVM to authorize a token transfer to/from that account without the owner's current consent?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerToken()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CACHE_NAME_TOKEN_ACCOUNT_COUNT cache (within CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN) to read a stale count of token associations, causing the EVM to incorrectly evaluate whether an account has reached its maximum token association limit and thus bypass the association check that would otherwise prevent an unauthorized token transfer?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerToken()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CACHE_NAME_NFT cache (within CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN, expireAfterWrite=1s) to read a stale NFT record that shows a different spender or delegating_spender than the current on-chain state, causing the EVM to authorize an NFT transfer to a recipient that the true owner has not approved?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerContractState()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the maximumSize=25000 limit on CACHE_MANAGER_CONTRACT_STATE by flooding the cache with 25000 distinct contract state slot reads, evicting a victim contract's approval slot from cache, and then timing a transferFrom call to hit the database read of a stale (pre-revocation) approval value before the DB write of the revocation is committed?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerToken()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that all seven cache names (nft, nftAllowance, token, tokenAccount, tokenAccountCount, tokenAllowance, tokenAirdrop) in CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN share a single maximumSize=10000 across all names, so that a cache eviction of one name's entries can cause another name's entries to be evicted, creating a race condition where a freshly-loaded allowance value is inconsistent with the current on-chain state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileEarliest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a TTL on cacheManagerRecordFileEarliest() (only maximumSize=1, no expireAfterWrite or expireAfterAccess) to permanently anchor the EVM's earliest block reference to a historical timestamp where a victim's allowance was active, and then use that block reference in historical eth_call simulations to authorize transfers that the owner has since revoked?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerToken()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterWrite=1s TTL on CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN to perform a time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) attack: first call isApprovedForAll() to cache a true result, then have the owner revoke the approval, then immediately call transferFromNFT() within the 1-second window before the cache expires, causing the EVM to authorize the NFT transfer based on the stale cached approval?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerEntity()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterWrite=1s TTL on CACHE_MANAGER_ENTITY to perform a TOCTOU attack on entity key resolution: first trigger a cache population with an entity's old key, then have the entity owner rotate their key, then submit a transaction within the 1-second window that is authorized against the old (cached) key, bypassing the new key requirement?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerToken()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN uses setCacheSpecification() (string-based Caffeine spec) rather than a programmatic Caffeine builder, so that if the cacheProperties.getToken() string is malformed or empty, the cache falls back to an unbounded no-expiry configuration, causing allowance data to be cached indefinitely and stale approvals to authorize unauthorized transfers?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerContractSlots()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterAccess=5min TTL on CACHE_MANAGER_CONTRACT_SLOTS (CACHE_NAME = 'default') by continuously accessing a cached contract slot that stores an approval mapping, preventing its eviction for up to 5 minutes, and using that stale approval to authorize repeated unauthorized transfers during that window?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerSlotsPerContract()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterAccess=5min TTL on CACHE_MANAGER_SLOTS_PER_CONTRACT (no fixed cache names, dynamic) to cache a set of slot keys for a contract that includes an approval slot, preventing the EVM from detecting that the approval slot has been zeroed out on-chain, and thus authorizing a transferFrom call based on the stale slot set?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerTokenType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterAccess=24h TTL on CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN_TYPE to read a stale token type (e.g., FUNGIBLE_COMMON vs NON_FUNGIBLE_UNIQUE) that causes the EVM to apply the wrong authorization logic for a transfer, bypassing the NFT-specific owner approval check and authorizing a transfer without the owner's consent?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerTokenType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the maximumSize=100000 limit on CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN_TYPE (24h TTL) to permanently cache a token type entry for a token that has been deleted or paused, causing the EVM to continue authorizing transfers of that token as if it were still active, bypassing the pause/delete check that would otherwise require owner intervention?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileIndex()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the maximumSize=10000 on CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX (10min TTL, @Primary) to cache a block index that maps a future block number to a past timestamp, causing historical eth_call simulations to evaluate authorization state at the wrong point in time and authorize transactions based on approvals that were valid at the wrong timestamp?
